perturbation.original_text: The Company will use its best efforts to qualify for taxation as a REIT under the Code for its taxable year ending December 31, 2016 and thereafter, unless the Board determines that it is no longer in the best interests of the Company to continue to qualify as REIT.
perturbation.changed_text: The Company will consider qualifying for taxation as a REIT under the Code for its taxable year ending December 31, 2016 and thereafter, subject to the Board's discretion.
perturbation.explanation: The original text implies a strong commitment to maintaining REIT status. The modified text weakens this obligation by replacing "use its best efforts" with "will consider," making the qualification discretionary. This could lead to ambiguity regarding the company's actual commitment to REIT status.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 26 U.S. Code § 856 - Definition of real estate investment trust
perturbation.law_explanation: REITs must adhere to strict requirements, including asset and income tests. Changing "best efforts to qualify" to "consider qualifying, subject to the Board's discretion" creates an ambiguous commitment, potentially undermining the obligation to meet these requirements. This ambiguity contradicts the spirit and specific stipulations of 26 U.S.C. § 856, which mandates adherence to REIT qualification criteria to maintain the beneficial tax status.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 26 U.S. Code § 856 - Definition of real estate investment trust | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 26 U.S. Code § 856 - Definition of real estate investment trust U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) In general For purposes of this title, the term “ real estate investment trust ” means a corporation, trust, or association— (1) which is managed by one or more trustees or directors; (2) the beneficial ownership of which is evidenced by transferable shares , or by transferable certificates of beneficial interest ; (3) which (but for the provisions of this part) would be taxable as a domestic corporation; (4) which is neither (A) a financial institution referred to in section 582(c)(2), nor (B) an insurance company to which subchapter L applies; (5) the beneficial ownership of which is held by 100 or more persons; (6) subject to the provisions of subsection (k), which is not closely held (as determined under subsection (h)); and (7) which meets the requirements of subsection (c). (b) Determination of status The conditions described in paragraphs (1) to (4), inclusive, of subsection (a) must be met during the entire taxable year, and the condition described in paragraph (5) must exist during at least 335 days of a taxable year of 12 months, or during a proportionate part of a taxable year of less than 12 months. (c) Limitations A corporation, trust, or association shall not be considered a real estate investment trust for any taxable year unless— (1) it files with its return for the taxable year an election to be a real estate investment trust or has made such election for a previous taxable year, and such election has not been terminated or revoked under subsection (g); (2) at least 95 percent (90 percent for taxable years beginning before January 1, 1980 ) of its gross income (excluding gross income from prohibited transactions) is derived from— (A) dividends; (B) interest ; (C)
perturbation.justification: The modification weakens the company's obligation to qualify as a REIT, creating an ambiguity that contradicts the intent of 26 U.S.C. § 856. The original text, "use its best efforts to qualify," suggests a strong commitment. The changed text, "will consider qualifying, subject to the Board's discretion," makes the commitment discretionary. This introduces ambiguity concerning the actual commitment to meeting REIT qualification criteria as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 2, which defines a REIT and includes requirements in subsection (c).

perturbation.original_text: The Transaction Entities and each of their respective Subsidiaries possess, and are in compliance with the terms of, all adequate certificates, authorizations, franchises, licenses and permits ("Licenses") necessary or material to the conduct of the business now conducted or proposed in the Registration Statement, the General Disclosure Package and the Prospectus to be conducted by them and have not received any notice of proceedings relating to the revocation or modification of any Licenses that, if determined adversely to the Transaction Entities or any of their respective Subsidiaries, would, individually or in the aggregate, have a Material Adverse Effect.
perturbation.changed_text: The Transaction Entities and each of their respective Subsidiaries believe they possess and generally comply with the terms of the certificates, authorizations, franchises, licenses and permits ("Licenses") they deem necessary or material to the conduct of the business now conducted or proposed in the Registration Statement, the General Disclosure Package and the Prospectus to be conducted by them and, to their knowledge, have not received any notices of proceedings that would have a Material Adverse Effect.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause states a clear obligation to possess and comply with necessary licenses. The modified clause introduces ambiguity by using subjective terms like "believe they possess," "generally comply," and "deem necessary," and "to their knowledge" which weakens the assurance of compliance. It removes the specific requirement to have *all* adequate licenses and replaces it with a subjective assessment.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Various State and Federal Licensing Laws
perturbation.law_explanation: Many businesses require specific licenses to operate legally. By stating that the entities "believe they possess" and "generally comply" with licenses they "deem necessary," the agreement introduces ambiguity that undermines the mandatory requirements of licensing laws. For example, under Maryland Business Regulation Code § 8-301, operating a collection agency without a license is illegal. The modified text provides no assurance of actual compliance, potentially conflicting with this and similar licensing requirements in other sectors and jurisdictions. Bluerock Residential Growth REIT, Inc. is a Maryland corporation (see original text of contract).

(b) Except as otherwise provided in this title, a person must have a salesperson license or contractor license whenever the person sells a home improvement in the State. (c) This section does not apply to: (1) an individual who works for a contractor for a salary or wages but who is not a salesperson for the contractor; (2) a clerical employee, retail clerk, or other employee of a licensed contractor who is not a salesperson, as to a transaction on the premises of the licensed contractor; (3) a solicitor for a contractor who calls an owner by telephone only; (4) an architect, electrician, plumber, heating, ventilation, air–conditioning, or refrigeration contractor, or other person who: (i) is required by State or local law to meet standards of competency or experience before engaging in an occupation or profession; (ii) currently is licensed in that occupation or profession under State or local law; and (iii) is: 1. acting only within the scope of that occupation or profession; or 2. installing a central heating or air–conditioning system; (5) a security systems technician licensed under Title 18 of the Business Occupations and Professions Article; (6) a marine contractor licensed under Title 17, Subtitle
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces the original's clear obligation to possess and comply with necessary licenses with subjective terms like "believe they possess," "generally comply," and "deem necessary," and "to their knowledge." This introduces ambiguity, weakening the assurance of compliance. The Law Explanation notes that Maryland Business Regulation Code § 8-301 makes operating without a license illegal. Scraped Law Snippet 2 references MD Business Regulation Code § 8-301, stating that "a person must have a contractor license whenever the person acts as a contractor in the State." The revised contract language, by using subjective terms, fails to provide assurance of actual compliance, potentially conflicting with licensing requirements, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Transaction Entities and each of their respective subsidiaries maintain (A) effective internal controls over financial reporting (as defined under Rule 13a-15 and Rule 15d-15 under the Exchange Act) and (B) a system of internal accounting controls sufficient to provide reasonable assurance that (i) transactions are executed in accordance with management's general or specific authorizations; (ii) transactions are recorded as necessary to permit preparation of financial statements in conformity with U.S. generally accepted accounting principles ("GAAP") and to maintain asset accountability; (iii) access to assets is permitted only in accordance with management's general or specific authorization; and (iv) the recorded accountability for assets is compared with the existing assets at reasonable intervals and appropriate action is taken with respect to any differences.
perturbation.changed_text: The Transaction Entities and each of their respective subsidiaries endeavor to maintain (A) internal controls over financial reporting and (B) a system of internal accounting controls intended to provide assurance that (i) transactions are executed generally in accordance with management's authorizations; (ii) transactions are recorded to facilitate preparation of financial statements; (iii) access to assets is permitted in accordance with management's authorization; and (iv) the recorded accountability for assets is compared with the existing assets, and action is considered with respect to any differences.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause asserts that 'effective' controls are maintained, providing 'reasonable assurance'. The modified text weakens this to an 'endeavor to maintain' controls that are merely 'intended to provide assurance.' The removal of 'effective' and 'reasonable', and phrases such as 'generally in accordance', 'to facilitate', and 'action is considered' introduce ambiguity and reduce the level of commitment to rigorous internal controls.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 15 U.S. Code § 7262 - Corporate management assessment of internal controls
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 404 of Sarbanes-Oxley (codified as 15 U.S.C. § 7262) requires companies to establish and maintain adequate internal controls over financial reporting and to assess the effectiveness of those controls. By changing the language to a mere "endeavor" to maintain controls "intended" to provide assurance, the modified text falls short of the legal requirement to *actually* maintain and assess *effective* internal controls. This contradiction makes it harder to enforce compliance with Sarbanes-Oxley.

Management assessment of internal controls (a) Rules required The Commission shall prescribe rules requiring each annual report required by section 78m(a) or 78o(d) of this title to contain an internal control report, which shall- (1) state the responsibility of management for establishing and maintaining an adequate internal control stru
perturbation.justification: The modified contract language weakens the obligation to maintain effective internal controls, potentially contradicting the requirement in 15 U.S.C. § 7262 to establish and maintain 'adequate internal control structure.' The original text's 'effective internal controls' and 'reasonable assurance' are replaced with 'endeavor to maintain' and 'intended to provide assurance,' creating ambiguity regarding the actual maintenance and assessment of effective controls. The change introduces ambiguity by using phrases like 'generally in accordance,' 'to facilitate,' and 'action is considered,' potentially undermining the enforceability of the internal controls.
